ElectronicHazard.title: Electronic Hazard
ElectronicHazard.description: Involves common household and office electronics that are either improperly placed, unsecured, or operating with damaged enclosures. These devices contain sensitive circuitry, high-voltage power supplies, and in some cases, heavy glass panels or rapid-moving parts that require stable environments to operate safely.
ElectronicHazard.reason: This is High urgency because these items become dangerous during disasters. In an earthquake, unsecured screens can topple and shatter, while a typhoon causes rain to enter vents, leading to explosive short-circuits. In a fire, plastic housings release toxic smoke and internal components can pop, rapidly spreading flames to nearby furniture.

Earthquake.fix_1: Move top-heavy items like monitors and flat-screen TVs to the floor or lower shelves to prevent them from falling.

Earthquake.fix_2: Use nylon quake straps or industrial-strength Velcro to anchor desktops and TVs to the wall or heavy furniture.

Earthquake.fix_3: Install specialized anti-tip brackets for large screens and ensure all desktop setups are placed on non-slip rubber mats.

Typhoon.fix_1: Unplug all electronics and move them away from windows and off the floor to a height of at least 1 meter.

Typhoon.fix_2: Cover essential electronics with thick plastic sheeting if there are signs of roof leaks, ensuring the devices are fully powered down first.

Typhoon.fix_3: Use surge protectors with high joule ratings for all appliances to prevent circuit damage from power fluctuations.

Fire.fix_1: Maintain at least 10cm of space around the vents of desktops and TVs to prevent overheating and spontaneous ignition.

Fire.fix_2: Clear away tangled wiring and dust buildup behind fans and computers, as dust acts as kindling for electrical sparks.

Fire.fix_3: Replace any device with a frayed power cord or a cracked casing immediately to ensure all electrical current stays contained.

ElevatedBreakables.title: Elevated Breakables
ElevatedBreakables.description: Fragile items like glass vases, heavy bottles, or ceramic decor placed on high shelves or unsecured surfaces. These free-standing objects rely entirely on gravity to stay in place and lack any protective barriers.
ElevatedBreakables.reason: This is High urgency because these items become dangerous projectiles during disasters. In an earthquake, they topple and shatter into sharp debris, while typhoon vibrations can cause them to slide off surfaces. In a fire, heat can cause glass to explode, creating hidden hazards in smoky conditions.

Earthquake.fix_1: Apply museum wax or earthquake putty to the base of all breakable decor to bond them firmly to the shelf surface.

Earthquake.fix_2: Move the heaviest or sharpest glass items to the lowest possible shelves to reduce the distance they could fall.

Earthquake.fix_3: Install 'shelf lips' or guard rails on open shelving to prevent items from sliding over the edge during a shake.

Typhoon.fix_1: Relocate glass bottles and vases away from windows to prevent them from being knocked over by wind pressure or broken glass.

Typhoon.fix_2: Place anti-slip rubber mats on shelves to increase friction and stop items from vibrating toward the edge during high winds.

Typhoon.fix_3: Store valuable or dangerous breakables inside latched cabinets to ensure they remain contained if the building vibrates.

Fire.fix_1: Clear all glassware from hallway tables or primary exit paths to ensure a clear route during low-visibility evacuations.

Fire.fix_2: Ensure heavy wall-mounted mirrors or glass frames are screwed into wall studs rather than hanging on wire that could melt.

Fire.fix_3: Switch to tempered glass for decorative items in high-traffic areas, as it breaks into small, less dangerous nuggets rather than sharp shards.

ExposedBreakers.title: Exposed Circuit Breakers
ExposedBreakers.description: Electrical service panels or switch boxes where the protective safety cover is missing. This leaves internal high-voltage copper bars and terminal lugs accessible to accidental touch or water splashes.
ExposedBreakers.reason: This is Critical because the main electrical hub is undefended. In an earthquake, shaking causes high-voltage arcs. In a typhoon, wind-driven rain causes explosive short-circuits. In a fire, the lack of a cover allows sparks to leap to walls and ceilings instantly.

Earthquake.fix_1: Switch off the main breaker to de-energize the panel immediately if severe shaking begins to prevent internal arcing.

Earthquake.fix_2: Ensure the panel box is bolted securely into concrete wall studs so it does not tear loose during a tremor.

Earthquake.fix_3: Replace any brittle or sun-damaged plastic housings that might shatter and expose wires during a shake.

Typhoon.fix_1: Cover the exposed panel with a non-conductive, waterproof shroud if there are leaks in the roof or 'dirty kitchen' area.

Typhoon.fix_2: If located in a flood-prone area, work with a licensed electrician to relocate the panel at least 1.5 meters above the floor.

Typhoon.fix_3: Install a NEMA-rated waterproof enclosure (metal or thick plastic) to block wind-driven rain from entering the breaker gaps.

Fire.fix_1: Clear all 'abukay' or clutter like cardboard boxes and cleaning rags from the 1-meter space around the panel.

Fire.fix_2: Install a proper metal 'dead front' cover to ensure any internal electrical sparks are trapped inside the box.

Fire.fix_3: Keep a Class C (CO2) or dry chemical fire extinguisher near the kitchen or hallway where the breakers are located.

ExposedCeilingLights.title: Exposed Ceiling Lights
ExposedCeilingLights.description: Lighting fixtures with broken bulbs, missing diffusers, or dangling 'octopus' wires. These overhead hazards are often poorly insulated and prone to environmental damage.
ExposedCeilingLights.reason: This is Critical because overhead faults drop hazards directly onto residents. In an earthquake, swinging bulbs shatter and fall, while typhoon leaks cause water to enter live sockets. In a fire, exposed wires allow flames to spread rapidly across wooden ceiling trusses.

Earthquake.fix_1: Replace heavy hanging glass chandeliers with lightweight LED surface-mount fixtures that won't swing violently during tremors.

Earthquake.fix_2: Ensure all ceiling junction boxes are screwed tightly into the joists so the entire fixture does not fall on someone below.

Earthquake.fix_3: Use plastic bulb guards or 'cages' for bare bulbs to prevent the glass from scattering if the bulb hits a nearby object.

Typhoon.fix_1: Seal any roof leaks immediately to prevent water from traveling down the electrical conduit and reaching the light socket.

Typhoon.fix_2: Install 'IP65' rated waterproof outdoor lights for the dirty kitchen or balcony to resist high-humidity and wind-driven rain.

Typhoon.fix_3: Shorten long dangling wires so they do not sway and pull on the ceiling connections during high-velocity typhoon winds.

Fire.fix_1: Remove 'octopus' wiring where multiple lights are spliced into one thin wire, as this causes overheating and ceiling fires.

Fire.fix_2: Replace exposed incandescent bulbs with LEDs, which run much cooler and are less likely to ignite nearby dust or cobwebs.

Fire.fix_3: Wrap any exposed wire splices with high-quality electrical tape and tuck them safely inside a fire-rated plastic junction box.

HeavyWoodenFurniture.title: Heavy Wooden Furniture
HeavyWoodenFurniture.description: Unsecured top-heavy furniture like traditional Filipino aparadors, wardrobes, and massive solid-wood tables. These items lack wall anchors and can easily tip over during ground movement.
HeavyWoodenFurniture.reason: This is High urgency because these items become lethal crushing hazards. In an earthquake, they topple and block bedroom exits, while in a flood, they can shift and pin residents. In a fire, solid wood acts as a massive fuel source that sustains high temperatures for long periods.

Earthquake.fix_1: Secure the top of tall aparadors to the wall using heavy-duty L-brackets or nylon furniture straps screwed into the wall studs.

Earthquake.fix_2: Place the heaviest items, like thick blankets or stacked plates, at the very bottom of the closet to lower the center of gravity.

Earthquake.fix_3: Install 'wedge' levelers under the front feet of the wardrobe so it leans slightly back toward the wall rather than forward.

Typhoon.fix_1: Move heavy wooden tables away from glass windows to prevent them from being pushed into the glass by strong wind gusts.

Typhoon.fix_2: Empty the contents of bottom drawers if flooding is expected to prevent the wood from absorbing water and becoming twice as heavy.

Typhoon.fix_3: If the furniture has wheels or rollers, lock them or place them in 'caster cups' to prevent the item from rolling across the room during vibrations.

Fire.fix_1: Rearrange heavy closets so they do not sit directly in front of windows or doors that serve as secondary fire escape routes.

Fire.fix_2: Keep a 1-meter clearance between wooden furniture and heat sources like electric fans, rice cookers, or gas stoves in the kitchen.

Fire.fix_3: Apply fire-retardant varnish to antique wooden furniture to slow down the rate at which the wood catches fire during an emergency.

OpenFlameHazards.title: Open Flame Hazards
OpenFlameHazards.description: Active, unshielded flames used for lighting or pest control, such as candles, gaseras, and mosquito coils. These are high-risk ignition sources when placed near flammable household materials.
OpenFlameHazards.reason: This is Critical because a single tip-over can ignite a home in minutes. In an earthquake, flames fall and start fires instantly, while typhoon drafts can blow curtains into active coils. During a fire, these serve as the primary ignition points that lead to total structural loss.

Earthquake.fix_1: Switch from wax candles and gaseras to battery-operated LED lanterns or rechargeable 'brownout' lights that cannot start a fire if they fall.

Earthquake.fix_2: If using a flame, place it inside a heavy glass hurricane lantern or a deep ceramic bowl to contain the fire if the unit tips over.

Earthquake.fix_3: Extinguish all open flames immediately at the first sign of ground shaking to prevent a post-earthquake fire.

Typhoon.fix_1: Place mosquito coils (katol) inside a metal 'katol' box with a lid to prevent wind gusts from blowing hot embers onto the floor or curtains.

Typhoon.fix_2: Ensure all candles or gas lamps are kept at least 1 meter away from windows and 'kulambo' (mosquito nets) that can be moved by the wind.

Typhoon.fix_3: Secure gasera tanks to a heavy, non-flammable base like a cement tile so they cannot be knocked over by sudden vibrations or wind.

Fire.fix_1: Never leave a mosquito coil or candle burning unattended or while sleeping; always double-check that they are fully extinguished.

Fire.fix_2: Keep matches and lighters in a high, metal container out of reach of children and away from the heat of the 'dirty kitchen' stove.

Fire.fix_3: Replace traditional gas lamps with solar-powered lamps which provide safe, flame-free lighting even during extended rainy seasons.
